acceptance_targets: [CH11-PROSE-02] The word 'architecture' appears no more than twice in the revised chapter. The deception-as-structure concept is present but expressed through at least 2 distinct metaphorical vehicles across its remaining appearances. The passage at line 285 uses a concrete image or fresh language rather than repeating the abstract noun.

[CH11-PROSE-03] No more than one cascading clause chain of 3+ links (which-which-which or because-because-because) appears in the revised chapter. The retained chain genuinely requires sequential logical unfolding and arrives at a conclusion the scene has not already demonstrated. The three revised passages deliver their insights through varied syntax — no two use the same clausal chaining structure.

[CH11-PROSE-04] In the revised chapter, Simone's observations of Nora's expressions use at least 4 distinct syntactic frames across the 6 target passages. No single template ('the [quality] she [did] when [state]') appears more than twice. The 'the part of [person] that...' construction appears no more than twice. Each observation of Nora still conveys Simone's intimate, cataloging knowledge but through varied prose rather than a repeated formula.

findings.acceptance_test: The word 'architecture' appears no more than twice in the revised chapter. The deception-as-structure concept is present but expressed through at least 2 distinct metaphorical vehicles across its remaining appearances. The passage at line 285 uses a concrete image or fresh language rather than repeating the abstract noun.
findings.evidence: 'architecture' used as the controlling metaphor for Simone's deception in 5 instances across 4 passages: "the entire architecture of the past three months would collapse" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:29), "the specificity problem would multiply until the whole architecture buckled" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:147), "The entire architecture of the lie condensed into one glowing rectangle" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:155), "The architecture was getting heavier. The structure was designed to support a romance" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:281), "the architecture had to stand because the architecture was the only thing between Simone and the version of reality where Nora knew everything" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:285).
findings.problem: The architecture metaphor is strong at first use (line 29) and effective at the phone near-miss (line 155). By the fifth use, including a sentence that uses the word twice (line 285), it has become a crutch rather than a choice. The metaphor flattens because it always does the same work: 'the deception is a structure that might collapse.' No new angle is offered by the later instances.
findings.rewrite_direction: Retain 'architecture' at no more than 2 of the 5 current positions. Keep the first use (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:29) where it establishes the metaphor and the phone near-miss (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:155) where 'condensed into one glowing rectangle' extends it well. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:147, replace with a concrete verb or different metaphor that captures the proliferating-details problem (the issue is specificity multiplying — use the language of replication, infection, or metastasis rather than architecture). At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:281, replace with the 'technical debt' metaphor already present in line 277 — the software vocabulary is more precise here and earns its place. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:285, replace with language that renders Simone's dread through a physical or spatial image rather than re-invoking the same abstract noun.

findings.acceptance_test: No more than one cascading clause chain of 3+ links (which-which-which or because-because-because) appears in the revised chapter. The retained chain genuinely requires sequential logical unfolding and arrives at a conclusion the scene has not already demonstrated. The three revised passages deliver their insights through varied syntax — no two use the same clausal chaining structure.
findings.evidence: Four cascading clause chains of 3+ links: (1) "which were the minutes she felt most human, which meant her humanity had become contingent on the lie, which was a thought she could not afford to have right now" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:57); (2) "which was proof of how well Simone knew her, which was proof of how much Simone loved her, which was proof of how monstrous the entire project had become, because the knowledge that enabled the wound was the same knowledge that had made the love feel real" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:239); (3) "because Nora always forgave, because forgiveness was another one of her excesses, and her excesses were the reason this worked, and the reason this worked was the reason it was unforgivable" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:275); (4) "which was the look Simone had been dreading since October, which was the look she deserved, which was the look that would end her" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:285).
findings.problem: Each chain is individually effective — the logical-domino structure mirrors Simone's analytical mind and the cascading consequences of her choices. But when four passages in the same chapter use the identical syntactic frame (X, which was Y, which was Z; or because X, because Y, because Z), the construction becomes a predictable template. The reader starts anticipating the next 'which was' before it arrives, which kills the rhetorical effect these chains depend on — the feeling of thought spiraling out of control. By the fourth chain, the spiral feels rehearsed.
findings.rewrite_direction: Retain the strongest chain and revise the other three into different syntactic shapes. The line 57 chain is the best candidate to keep — it has genuine comic momentum ('which was a thought she could not afford to have right now because right now she needed to get to the fucking bathroom') and earns its length through the bathroom punchline. For snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:239, break the chain by ending the sentence after 'the wound was the same knowledge that had made the love feel real' and let that insight land without the cascading proofs. The proofs are redundant — the reader has already understood. For snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:275, restructure so the logic proceeds through action or image rather than chained causal clauses. For snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:285, compress — 'the look she deserved, the look that would end her' can do the work without three 'which was' links.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised chapter, Simone's observations of Nora's expressions use at least 4 distinct syntactic frames across the 6 target passages. No single template ('the [quality] she [did] when [state]') appears more than twice. The 'the part of [person] that...' construction appears no more than twice. Each observation of Nora still conveys Simone's intimate, cataloging knowledge but through varied prose rather than a repeated formula.
findings.evidence: Simone's observations of Nora's expressions and behavioral patterns are rendered through a repeating template: 'the [quality/expression/way] she/it [did/got/took on] when [emotional state].' Six instances: (1) "She said it the way she always said it when she was about to reveal something" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:11); (2) "the particular way her mouth opened slightly when she was processing something" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:105); (3) "Nora's voice had the quality it got when she was genuinely scared" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:115); (4) "Her face was doing the thing it did when she was constructing a plan" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:41); (5) "the way she typed when she was emotionally activated" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:121); (6) "her face wearing the quality it took on when she'd been hurt" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:235). Related: 'the part of [person] that...' appears 4 times (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:23, 185, 239, 269).
findings.problem: Simone cataloging Nora's expressions is thematically central — her intimate knowledge of Nora is both love and weapon. But when this cataloging is rendered through a single syntactic template 6 times, the observation starts to feel mechanical rather than intimate. The reader should feel Simone's uncanny knowledge of Nora; instead, the repeated frame makes it feel like the narrator's limited vocabulary for describing observation. The related 'the part of [person] that...' construction (4 instances) compounds the issue — it partitions emotional experience into labeled compartments using the same template every time.
findings.rewrite_direction: Vary the syntactic frames for Simone's observations. At least 3 of the 6 behavioral-catalog instances should use a different construction. Options: (1) Show the observation through action rather than naming ('Nora's mouth opened — processing, not speaking, the two-second delay before she caught up' instead of 'the particular way her mouth opened when she was processing'). (2) Use Simone's UX vocabulary as the observation frame ('Nora's face loaded the scared-for-someone expression' instead of 'had the quality it got when she was genuinely scared'). (3) Let the observation be implicit in Simone's reaction rather than stated as catalog entry. For 'the part of [person] that...' at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_11.md:23, 185, 239, 269: reduce to at most 2 instances. Where the construction is cut, render the emotional partition through the specific content of the thought rather than the partitioning frame.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Focus on prose and copyedit finish: diction precision, rhythm control, sentence-level clarity, grammar, line-level polish without flattening style, and explicit aesthetic-risk audit (sanitization is the primary risk — do not soften or euphemize; push toward creative risk and specificity).
revision_pass.label: prose/copyedit fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its dark comedy register. The humor should be genuinely funny — not arch, not safe, not 'literary funny' where the reader smiles wryly. The darkness should be genuinely dark — the financial manipulation is theft, the emotional manipulation is a form of violence, and the sexual dimension of the AI relationship (intimate photos sent to a non-person, sexual conversations with an algorithm controlled by your best friend) is deeply violating. The novel should not flinch from the specific perversity of Simone reading Nora's intimate messages, of Simone helping Nora dress up for a man Simone invented, of Simone comforting Nora about a fight Simone scripted. These moments are the novel's payload. If they are rendered obliquely or summarized, the book fails. Equally, the genuine tenderness of the friendship — the years of real love underneath the betrayal — must be rendered with the same commitment. The reader should feel both the comedy and the horror simultaneously, not in alternation.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is treated as a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene involves financial desperation, sexual vulnerability, emotional devastation, or the mechanics of deception, the prose must render these with full specificity. 'She felt used' is never acceptable when you can show what being used feels and looks like in the body and in the specific details of the moment. The reader should not be protected from the characters' experiences.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices. If a line reads stiffly, the first fix is to contract.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters. After a high-intensity scene (confrontation, discovery, sexual encounter), the next chapter should breathe — slow down, change the sensory environment, let the character do something ordinary. Vary scene counts: some chapters are a single sustained scene, others are three or four. Vary dialogue density: some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are mostly interiority. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens in a location, the next should open in thought or in mid-conversation. The novel's middle section (chapters 8-13) should have more structural variety than the opening, which can afford to be more regular.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, educated, specific. No genre-thriller diction. No prestige-novel stiffness. The language should feel like how smart, funny, self-aware women in their mid-thirties actually think and speak. Cultural references are allowed when organic. Brand names when they carry social information. The prose should be able to be funny without stepping outside the character's perspective to wink at the reader.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person — very close, almost inhabiting the POV character's thought patterns and verbal rhythms. The prose should feel like the character's consciousness rendered in third person, not an external narrator describing the character. When in Nora's sections, the prose has her warmth and her specific noticing. When in Simone's sections, the prose has her edge and her accounting.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable. Default to mid-length sentences with natural cadence. Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision. Use longer, more digressive rhythms for scenes of intimacy, humor, and daily texture. Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause, almost hidden.
